title: Chapter 13: The Hunter
content: Julian C closes the café at three.

Not four, which is when Mrs. O'Leary usually shuffles in for her scone. Not five, which is when the after-school crowd drifts through for hot chocolate. Three. The lock clicks shut and the OPEN sign flips to CLOSED and the street outside is still bathed in that lazy afternoon gold that makes Clonakilty look like a postcard of itself.

I watch him from behind the counter, a cold latte sweating rings onto the wood beneath my hands. He's at the window again. Curtains pulled almost closed, just a finger's width of gap, and his eye pressed to it like he's reading something written in the traffic.
A car slows outside. His shoulders seize up — a full-body flinch contained into two knots of muscle at the base of his neck. The car passes. His shoulders don't come down.
This is what it looks like, I realize. This is what it looks like when someone who's spent his whole life learning how to run finally has somewhere he doesn't want to leave.
He's choosing to stay. For me. And it might kill him.

The thought sits in my chest like a stone dropped into still water, the ripples spreading outward through my ribs, my throat, the backs of my eyes. I take a breath. The air in here is wrong — coffee gone cold and the sea-salt draft coming through the gap in the back door and underneath it all, faintly chemical, like the inside of a hospital. Like something sterile. Like something waiting.
"Come here," he says. Not looking away from the window.
I go.
He doesn't teach me café things.
"I'm serious, Avery. If someone comes through the front door —"
"Someone always comes through the front door. It's a café."

His jaw tightens. "If someone comes through the front door and it's not Mrs. O'Leary, you go out the back. Through the garden. Up the hill. Don't stop until you reach O'Malley's pub." He pauses. "Tell them J sent you. They'll hide you."
"Who's they?"

"The people who owe me." He says it like that's the end of the sentence, and I understand that for Julian C, debts are a currency he's been spending his whole life. Running up tabs he can't pay back. Calling in favors from people who won't forget.

He shows me how to lock the front door silently — thumb on the latch, turn slow, no click. He shows me how to check the windows for tampering — a single black hair taped across the frame at the corner, invisible unless you know where to look. "If the hair's broken, someone's been in."
"Is that actually reliable?"
"It's reliable enough that I'm still breathing."

He pulls a burner phone from behind the flour tin. Silver, cheap, the kind you buy at a gas station. "This stays off until you need it. Number's one — just the digit one. It calls my other burner. If I don't answer, you call O'Malley's and you tell them the same thing: J sent you."
"Julian —"

"Don't." He says it quietly. The way you'd say it to someone you're trying not to hurt. "Don't make this into something it isn't. I'm not being brave. I'm being practical. If he's coming for me, you need to know what to do when —" He stops. Corrects himself. "If."
If.
He's preparing me for an aftermath he doesn't expect to be part of. I taste it in the air between us — copper and sea salt and something burnt. Like the afterimage of a fire that hasn't started yet.
I take the burner phone. It's lighter than I expect. It weighs nothing at all.
I shift.

Not on purpose. The Fork app pulses against my thigh and I let it pull me — just for a second, just long enough to check. The transition is worse now. It's not the smooth bleed-through it used to be, soft edges and dreamlogic. It's violent. It's being dragged through a doorway by the collar, the frame catching on every rib.
Base reality. My kitchen. The familiar hum of the refrigerator and the particular quality of light through my window at — what time is it? Afternoon. Same light. Different world.
I pull out my phone. The Fork app opens and the number is there, waiting, red and accusing.
INTEGRITY: 65%.

Sixty-five. Down from sixty-eight. Down from seventy-three. Down from whatever number the universe started at before I started treating it like a buffet table, picking what I wanted and leaving the rest.
But now there's something else.

The timeline paths on the screen are flickering. Not just C, not just the ones I've visited — all of them. Lines that were distinct and separate are blurring at the edges, bleeding into each other like watercolors left in the rain. And overlaying everything, ghost-images: a flash of Julian A's office, Manhattan glass and steel, the sound of rain in Paris — Paris? — and the taste of Sancerre on my tongue, cold and mineral, though I'm standing in my kitchen in base reality and I haven't had wine in weeks.
The images layer over each other. For one horrible second I'm in three places at once and my body doesn't know which one is real. I gasp. The refrigerator hums. I'm back.
I shift to Timeline C.

The nausea hits like a fist. My knees buckle and I catch myself on the counter, the edge biting into my palms. My mouth floods with saliva and I swallow hard, once, twice, breathing through my nose. The café materializes around me — the smell of cold coffee, the sea draft, Julian C's hand on my elbow, steadying me.
"You shifted." Not a question.
"Just for a second."
"Your face is grey."
"The integrity's dropping. Sixty-five." I straighten up. The room tilts and then holds. "The timelines are blurring. I could see — other places. Other versions. They're overlapping."
Something moves behind his eyes. Not fear, exactly. Recognition. Like he's watching a structure fail in exactly the way he was told it would and he's out of time to be surprised.
"Then we're running out of road," he says.
He's on the bench across the street.
I see him because Julian sees him — the way Julian goes rigid, his whole body turning to stone in one clean contraction of muscle, his hand finding the curtain edge and going white-knuckled on it.

I look. A man on a bench. Reading a newspaper. Late forties, gray at the temples, a face designed to be forgotten. He's wearing a navy jacket, khaki trousers, shoes that look like they cost more than my rent but not so much more that you'd notice. He looks like a man waiting for a bus. He looks like a man who has all the time in the world.
Newspaper rustles. A page turns. The sound reaches me through the gap in the curtain, dry and deliberate as a sentence being finished.
"That's him," Julian says.

Two words. But the way he says them — quiet, certain, like a man who's been hunted before and knows what comes next. Like a man reading a diagnosis he's already suspected. Stage four. Terminal. Confirmed.

Marcus Harker. The name Julian said two days ago, the name that made him lock the café early and check the windows and bury a phone behind the flour. And here he is. Flesh and blood and navy jacket and a newspaper and the most terrifying thing about him is that he's doing nothing.
He's just watching.

Five minutes. I count them by the pulse in my throat. He turns a page. He looks at the café — not directly, not at the curtain-gap where Julian is standing, but at the building itself, the way you'd look at a house you're thinking of buying. Appraising. Patient.

The sea wind carries salt and something else — exhaust from the road, the faint yeast-smell from the bakery two doors down, and underneath it all, nothing. The man on the bench has no scent. Or maybe I'm too far away. Or maybe that's what danger smells like: nothing at all.

After five minutes, Harker folds his newspaper. Precise creases, no fumbling. He stands. He's taller than I expected — or maybe that's just what authority looks like when it unfolds from a bench. He doesn't look at the café again. He walks away, unhurried, and turns the corner and is gone.
That's worse.

That's so much worse. A man who makes a scene, who pounds on the door, who shouts and threatens — that's a man you can plan for. That's a man with impulse control issues and a timeline you can predict. But a man who sits and watches and leaves? That's a man who's confirmed the location. A man who will return with a plan.
Julian's hand finds mine.

Not soft. Not the careful touch from before — the one that asked permission, that hovered near my waist like a question. This is his fingers closing around mine, hard, desperate, gripping like I'm the edge of something and he's falling. His knuckles are white. Mine are too. It's not romantic. It's survival. It's the only time he's asked for help without words.
"I have to go tonight," he says. "He won't wait."
The café is quiet. Not peaceful-quiet. Waiting-quiet. The kind of quiet that holds its breath.

Julian is in the back, moving in that efficient way he has when the training takes over — checking windows, testing locks, folding a bag with clothes and cash and two passports I didn't know he had. He's not packing for both of us. He's packing for me. The burner phone is on the counter between us, and somewhere in the last hour it's become the most important object in the room.
I'm not going to use it the way he thinks.
The thought arrives fully formed, like it's been waiting. Not to call O'Malley's. Not to run. Something else entirely.
"Julian."
He doesn't stop moving. "Yeah?"
"You said you can't prove you didn't do it."
That stops him. He turns. His face is careful — the same careful expression he uses when he's scanning the street, reading for threats. Evaluating.
"What if I can help?"
He stares at me. And then he laughs — short, sharp, the kind of laugh that has no humor in it at all, just disbelief and exhaustion and the edge of something that might be grief.
"Avery. You can't. This isn't — this isn't a puzzle. This is a man who ruins people for a living, and he's very, very good at it, and you can't just —"
"I have information he doesn't."
That stops him differently. His eyes narrow. The training kicks in — I see it happen, the shift from exhausted to alert, the soldier surfacing like a diver coming up for air.
"What kind of information?"
"The kind that comes from other worlds."

I say it and I watch it land. Watch him process it — the absurdity first, then the possibility, then the calculation. Julian C doesn't know the specifics. He doesn't know about the Fork app, not really. He knows I have something, some way of knowing things I shouldn't, and he's been too smart or too scared to ask directly. But now I'm telling him: I have information from places he can't imagine, and I'm willing to use it.
For him.
Not for me. Not to escape into a softer timeline where this conversation never happened. For him.
"You'd do that?" he asks. "You'd — whatever it is you do, you'd use it for this?"
"I'm staying."

Three words. They land heavier than I expected. He hears something in them that I didn't plan — not just a choice but a declaration, a line drawn in the sand of a universe that's sixty-five percent intact and dropping. I'm not shifting away. I'm not running to O'Malley's. I'm not leaving him to face this alone.
He looks at me like I've lost my mind. And then — slowly, like sunrise over a battlefield, like the first light after a night you didn't expect to survive — he nods.
Outside, the street is empty. The bench is empty. The sea wind pushes through the gap in the curtains and I taste salt and cold and the faint metallic tang of a world coming apart.

Marcus Harker is hunting. Julian C wanted to run, and I chose to stay and fight. Neither of us has a plan. The integrity of the universe is at sixty-five percent and falling, and somewhere in the blur between timelines, a hundred other versions of this moment are flickering in and out of existence — a hundred other Averys, a hundred other choices, a hundred other outcomes I can see at the edges of my vision like ghosts pressing their faces to glass.
Harker will come back. He has all the time in the world.
We don't.
